one_line_takeaway: Certified-training losses (esp. Exp-IBP) and the authors' cheap new ForwAbs regularizer can be retuned to kill catastrophic overfitting and even out-robust multi-step PGD on CIFAR-10, but the gains evaporate on harder datasets like CIFAR-100 - the technique's promise is real but its current limits are severe.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10 the repurposing works: with the CNN-7 long schedule (Table 1), Exp-IBP beats PGD-5 at every radius, and attack-free pure IBP training beats even PGD-10 for epsilon >= 16/255. Certified training also cures catastrophic overfitting where single-step N-FGSM collapses - e.g. at epsilon=24/255 CIFAR-10 N-FGSM gets 0.23% AA while Exp-IBP/IBP/ForwAbs stay near 14-17%. But the benefit is dataset- and depth-dependent and does NOT generalize: on the harder CIFAR-100, multi-step PGD remains best at all radii and certified-training methods (and ForwAbs) underperform it and the ELLE regularizer. ForwAbs matches MTL-IBP's IBP-loss reduction at much lower runtime, but MTL-IBP itself is so unstable on deep networks that the authors drop it after it induced CO on SVHN. Tuning for empirical robustness also costs substantial clean accuracy.
core_contribution: An empirical study (TMLR 03/2025) on repurposing CERTIFIED-training techniques - originally designed to maximize formally VERIFIABLE robustness - toward improving EMPIRICAL robustness against adversarial examples. Two things are the paper's OWN contribution, and must be separated from the prior-work methods it evaluates: (1) the systematic finding that a pre-existing expressive loss, Exp-IBP (from De Palma et al. 2024b, NOT invented here), when its over-approximation coefficient alpha is explicitly tuned for empirical (not certified) robustness, can prevent catastrophic overfitting (CO) of single-step attacks and even match/beat some multi-step PGD baselines; and (2) a NEW, conceptually simple regularizer the authors introduce, ForwAbs (Forward pass in Absolute value), which upper-bounds the IBP interval width via a single forward pass through an auxiliary |W|-weighted linear network, approximating the CO-preventing effect of IBP-based losses at roughly the cost of one extra network evaluation instead of two. Exp-IBP, MTL-IBP, CC-IBP, SABR, IBP, FGSM/N-FGSM/PGD, and AutoAttack are all prior work used as tools/baselines, not paper contributions.
threat_model: Test-time evasion: an adversary crafts L-infinity-bounded perturbations (x' within an epsilon-ball of x, ||x'-x||_inf <= epsilon) to flip a classifier's prediction. The defense is robust training; robustness is measured empirically against strong attack suites (AutoAttack, PGD) and, additionally, certified via IBP bounds that hold against ANY attack in the epsilon-ball. The paper's angle is that empirical robustness alone can be defeated by unseen/stronger attacks, so it studies whether certified-training machinery transfers to the empirical setting, with catastrophic overfitting (single-step attacks that stay robust to single-step but collapse to ~0% under multi-step) as the central failure mode being defended against.
limitations: (1) Empirical only - no guarantees or theory that the observed CO-prevention transfers beyond the tested setups. (2) Severe failure on harder tasks: on CIFAR-100 certified training is beaten by both multi-step PGD and the ELLE local-linearity regularizer at all radii; the authors ascribe this to more classes / larger IBP bounds at initialization (PreActResNet18's IBP loss at init is >10^10 larger than the CNNs'). (3) Heavy clean-accuracy cost when tuning purely for AA robustness. (4) MTL-IBP is extremely sensitive on deep networks and can itself cause CO (excluded from later experiments). (5) Success depends jointly on network depth, schedule length, and dataset difficulty, limiting a clean prescription. (6) The paper explicitly does NOT claim to beat SOTA single-step adversarial training; it is a feasibility/limitations study.

quantitative_results: Table 1 (CNN-7, 160-epoch long schedule), CIFAR-10 epsilon=8/255: Exp-IBP AA 38.44%+-0.25 (IBP acc 0.00), IBP method AA 29.43%+-0.96, ForwAbs AA 37.92%+-0.08, PGD-10 AA 40.14%+-0.65, PGD-5 AA 37.32%+-0.71, N-FGSM AA 37.76%+-0.30

Table 1 CIFAR-10 epsilon=16/255: Exp-IBP AA 20.72%/IBP 20.55%, pure IBP AA 21.72%/IBP 21.62% (best), ForwAbs AA 17.61%, PGD-10 AA 21.13%, PGD-5 AA 18.19%, N-FGSM AA 7.13%+-11.43 (high variance from CO)

Table 1 CIFAR-10 epsilon=24/255: Exp-IBP AA 14.79%/IBP 14.74%, Exp-IBP-PGD-5 AA 16.45%, pure IBP AA 16.73%/IBP 16.56% (best), ForwAbs AA 14.39%, PGD-10 AA 15.14%, PGD-5 AA 11.47%, N-FGSM AA 0.23%+-0.64 (catastrophic overfitting)
Table 1 CIFAR-100 epsilon=24/255: multi-step PGD-10 best at AA 6.15%+-0.16, PGD-5 5.52%, ForwAbs 4.31%, pure IBP 3.78%, Exp-IBP 3.25%, N-FGSM 0.02% - certified training loses to multi-step PGD here
PreActResNet18 IBP loss at initialization is more than a factor 10^10 larger than on the CNN-7/CNN-5 architectures, explaining why deep nets resist certified training
In the de Jorge et al. PreActResNet18 setup (CIFAR-10 epsilon=8/255), Exp-IBP reaches almost 20% IBP certified accuracy while MTL-IBP attains negligible IBP accuracy

ForwAbs costs ~one forward pass vs ~two for full IBP-bound computation, and drives the IBP loss to roughly the same values as MTL-IBP with markedly lower per-epoch runtime; strong L1 weight regularization (coeff 0.04) fails to match this effect
baselines_compared: SABR and CC-IBP (prior expressive losses, appendix)
